learningObjectives.title: Interpret process, architecture, and comparison blocks

sections.title: Introduction to SAP GUI and SAP Fiori

sections.content: SAP provides two primary user interfaces for accessing business applications: SAP GUI (Graphical User Interface) and SAP Fiori. SAP GUI is the traditional desktop interface widely used by consultants and administrators, whereas SAP Fiori provides a modern, browser-based, role-oriented user experience.

sections.title: Key Concept
sections.content: SAP GUI focuses on transaction-based navigation, while SAP Fiori focuses on business tasks and user roles.
SAP GUI is the classic client application used to access SAP systems. It provides direct access to transaction codes (T-Codes), configuration screens and administrative functions.

sections.title: Main Components of SAP GUI
rows: Quick access to frequently used transactions

sections.content: SAP Fiori is SAP's modern user experience built on SAPUI5 technology. It provides role-based applications that simplify business processes and support desktop, tablet and mobile devices.

sections.title: SAP Fiori Features
rows: Apps shown according to user role
Works on desktop, tablet and mobile
Users can customize tiles and groups

sections.title: Real Project Example
sections.content: Transportation planners use SAP Fiori apps to monitor freight orders and transportation execution, while consultants still use SAP GUI for configuration and implementation activities.

sections.title: Best Practice
sections.content: Business users should primarily work with SAP Fiori apps, while functional consultants and administrators use SAP GUI for configuration and advanced system activities.

sections.title: Interview Tip
sections.content: Interviewers often ask why SAP still supports SAP GUI when SAP Fiori exists. Explain that SAP GUI is essential for configuration, administration and legacy transactions, while SAP Fiori improves the end-user experience.

sections.title: Common Mistake
sections.content: Do not say SAP GUI is obsolete. Both SAP GUI and SAP Fiori coexist in SAP S/4HANA, serving different purposes.

items: SAP GUI is widely used for configuration.
Both interfaces coexist in SAP S/4HANA.

businessBackground.title: Business Context
businessBackground.businessProblem: Study content often becomes hardcoded and difficult to scale.
businessBackground.solution: Move to schema-driven rendering with reusable typed blocks.
businessBackground.businessBenefit: Faster topic onboarding with lower maintenance overhead.

businessScenario.title: Scenario
businessScenario.businessProblem: Teams need to roll out study topics quickly.
businessScenario.currentProcess: Manual page updates for every module.
businessScenario.sapSolution: Use normalized dynamic blocks and shared renderer components.
businessScenario.expectedBenefits: Consistency, speed, and reliability across all modules.

steps.title: Author JSON
steps.description: Create topic metadata and content blocks

steps.title: Normalize
steps.description: Service converts nested schema to canonical blocks

steps.title: Persist
steps.description: Bookmarks, notes, progress, and completion saved

architecture.title: Renderer Architecture
architecture.description: Service normalization feeds the registry-based dynamic renderer.

sapObjects.title: Study Topic
sapObjects.definition: A canonical normalized unit rendered by the Study Notes page.

questions.question: What enables dynamic rendering?
questions.explanation: A registry maps block type to renderer component.

glossary.definition: A mapping between block type and renderer component.
Logic that converts varying input schema into canonical topic structure.

timeline.description: Set up canonical types and topic loading

timeline.title: Phase 2
timeline.description: Add dynamic block renderer and registry
